item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get this (what's with the timestamps?), but it's evocative enough to upvote on the writing strength alone.

item.title: Re: as below, so above
item.content:encoded: I hadn't noticed the timestamps at first. They're out of order, but reading them in the correct order didn't change anything for me unless I missed something.

item.title: as below, so above
item.content:encoded:
In the middle of a disturbing passage in which the bride attempts to make love to her mutilated lover, the tale ends. The conflict is not resolved, and the story comes to a halt. Shahrazad does not even deliver any resolution.

Also, I don't know if this was intentional or not, but I liked the detail with the timestamps for the second interview.

item.title: Dread-inducing and haunting
item.content:encoded:
I've been lurking on this site for a long time and made an account to try and decipher this one. Bottom line, it seems like the disappearing people are being pulled into a narrative, specifically, the more ancient and primal version of the Tale of the Ensorcelled Prince. They all work in the medical field, at least tangentially, are blind/missing their left eye, and are descending stairs when they are taken. Does the entity taking them mistake them for the lover, or are they selected for a superficial resemblance to the lover?

I'm going to guess furthermore that in the ancient version of the tale recovered from the library, the lover tries to distract the bride by telling her a story? That seems fitting both with the themes of the Thousand and One Nights and provides a nested narrative for 2747 to "cleanly" eliminate while leaving the rest of the story untouched (so far at least).

Aside from that, I have one quibble. It seems like the interviews with recovered victims are just long-winded word salad rants? They're well written, but it would help a lot to write a sentence or two establishing that they're possibly in a trance-state or otherwise drugged when they relate their experiences. I just can't see someone sitting in a brightly-lit room saying this to an interviewer.

Anyways hard +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is thoroughly creepy and gripping. I loved the Thousand and One Nights as a kid, and it's nice to see an SCP that references it. I'm really bugged that I can't draw a meaningful link to 2747, as it's one of my favourites, but alas. Maybe it will become clear after a re-read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not too familiar with the Thousand and One Nights, but I think not really understanding what was going on helped the creepy factor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ^ same here. I'm still upvoting, as like what i saw that i did understand :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't understand this skip, and I feel like there's something going over my head here. Novoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did you include so many on thousand and one nights references or am I seeing too deep into this?

Edit: I didn't read the last addendum. Disregard the first question, and take this upvote from me.

Loved it when you were shopping it around, love it now. The historicity combined with the vague sense of dread is great — plus, we always need more skips focused on non-Western ideas placed in non-Western settings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Min edited it to fix the mistake after I made that comment. Originally the containment procedures mentioned 2001 when the disappearances were 2008-2010. It now says 2010.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The containment procedures are talking about the last occurrence of a person disappearing, reappearing is separate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty damn fantastic. Wonderful dialogue and historical/literary tie ins, and the overall mystery is extremely enticing. My only real quibble is that the link to 2747 feels forced, unless there's a plot tie between the two that I'm not seeing beyond the fact that both involve narratives as part of their story. Also the procedures mention the last occurrence was 2001 but all the dates take place after that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
image credits: 1) interior of a fort, creative commons 3.0, wikicommons user Adam J.W.C. 2) public domain image of what im pretty sure ISNT middle persian, from the Bodleian Library

crit credits: sunnyclockwork, tufto, kalinin, thegreathippo, tygently, lilyflower

let's hope this sticks!
